Tooth Growth Dataset

Code 
# Load a dataset into the R Environment.
data(ToothGrowth)

# Look at the summary of the variables in the dataset.
summary(ToothGrowth)
      len        supp         dose      
 Min.   : 4.20   OJ:30   Min.   :0.500  
 1st Qu.:13.07   VC:30   1st Qu.:0.500  
 Median :19.25           Median :1.000  
 Mean   :18.81           Mean   :1.167  
 3rd Qu.:25.27           3rd Qu.:2.000  
 Max.   :33.90           Max.   :2.000
Code 
# Look at a frequency table of a categorical variable.
table(ToothGrowth$supp)

OJ VC 
30 30

This dataset investigates the effect of vitamin C on tooth growth in guinea pigs.

The two supplement delivery methods include OJ (Orange Juice) and VC (ascorbic acid).

It is a data frame with 60 observations and 3 variables.

Tooth Box Plot

Code 
# make sure to load the package ggplot2
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(data = ToothGrowth, 
       aes(x = supp,
           y = len)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    x = "Supplement Type",
    y = "Tooth Length"
  )

Two Sample T Test on OJ vs. VC

Code 
t.test(len ~ supp, data = ToothGrowth, var.equal = FALSE, alternative = "two.sided")

    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  len by supp
t = 1.9153, df = 55.309, p-value = 0.06063
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group OJ and group VC is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -0.1710156  7.5710156
sample estimates:
mean in group OJ mean in group VC 
        20.66333         16.96333

Results of T Test

  1. The default alpha value is 0.05, meaning the CI is 95%. Since the p-value is 0.06063, which is greater than 0.05, we can conclude that there is no statistically significant difference between these two data types. This means that the different types of vitamin C inputs do not seem to statistically meaningfully affect the tooth length of the guinea pigs.
  2. The default confidence interval is 95%, and 0 is within the interval (between -0.17 and 7.57), so the previous conclusion is correct.

# Tooth Growth Dataset

```{r}
# Load a dataset into the R Environment.
data(ToothGrowth)

# Look at the summary of the variables in the dataset.
summary(ToothGrowth)

# Look at a frequency table of a categorical variable.
table(ToothGrowth$supp)
```

*This dataset investigates the effect of vitamin C on tooth growth in guinea pigs.*

*The two supplement delivery methods include OJ (Orange Juice) and VC (ascorbic acid).*

*It is a data frame with 60 observations and 3 variables.*

# Tooth Box Plot

```{r}
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-cap: "Boxplots of tooth length by supplement delivery method."

# make sure to load the package ggplot2
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(data = ToothGrowth, 
       aes(x = supp,
           y = len)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    x = "Supplement Type",
    y = "Tooth Length"
  )
```

# Two Sample T Test on OJ vs. VC

```{r}
t.test(len ~ supp, data = ToothGrowth, var.equal = FALSE, alternative = "two.sided")

```

# Results of T Test

1.  The default alpha value is 0.05, meaning the CI is 95%. Since the p-value is 0.06063, which is greater than 0.05, we can conclude that there is no statistically significant difference between these two data types. This means that the different types of vitamin C inputs do not seem to statistically meaningfully affect the tooth length of the guinea pigs.
2.  The default confidence interval is 95%, and 0 is within the interval (between -0.17 and 7.57), so the previous conclusion is correct.
